docs.title: Aerial Robot Manual
docs.text:
A comprehensive reference for development, calibration, and deployment of the Articulated Aerial Robot (AAR) platform.

This manual covers everything from low-level STM32 firmware development to real-machine deployment, organized so that common knowledge and robot-specific quirks are easy to find separately.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text:
• Robots

    Platform-specific guides: from simulation to real-machine deployment.

    Dragon · Spidar · Beetle

  • Hardware

    Spinal, Neuron, ESC, servo, sensors, upper computer setup.

    Hardware Overview

  • Calibration

    Motor test, ESC calibration, servo zero-point, IMU calibration.

    Calibration Procedures

  • Firmware Development

    STM32 build environment, debug with VS Code + OpenOCD.

    Dev Environment

  • Simulation

    Gazebo and MuJoCo simulation setup and usage.

    Gazebo Quickstart

  • Version History

    Hardware PCB versions, firmware versions, compatibility matrix.

    Hardware Versions

docs.title: Documentation Status
docs.text:
Section Status Owner Firmware Dev Environment Done — Spinal IMU Calibration Done — Hardware Versions Partial — Dragon Robot Stub — Spidar Robot Stub — Beetle Robot Stub — Motor Test Stub — VIM4 Setup Stub — Gazebo Simulation Stub — ROS 2 Planned —

How to contribute

See the Contributing Guide for writing conventions, image guidelines, and how to add a new robot or controller to this manual.

docs.title: Contributing Guide
docs.text: This guide covers how to write, update, and maintain documentation in this manual.

docs.title: Common vs. Robot-Specific
docs.text:
hardware/common/        ← Hardware behavior that is the same regardless of which robot it's on
hardware/robot_specific/← Assembly quirks or BOM differences specific to one robot
robots/<name>/          ← User-facing guide for operating that specific robot
                          (links to common/ for shared procedures)

Rule of thumb: If the same step applies to Dragon, Spidar, and Beetle identically, it goes in common/. If it's only true for one robot, it goes in robot_specific/ or robots/<name>/.

docs.title: Controller Variants
docs.text:
Each robot can have multiple controllers developed by different team members. Place controller-specific docs under:

robots/<name>/controllers/<controller_name>/
├── overview.md     ← What it does, who maintains it, which branch/repo
├── simulation.md   ← How to run in sim
└── tuning.md       ← Parameters and tuning guide

The robots/<name>/controllers/README.md acts as the index listing all available controllers and their status.

docs.title: Page Template
docs.text:
Every new page should follow this structure:

---
tags:
  - dragon          # Which robots this applies to (dragon/spidar/beetle/all)
  - spinal-h7v2     # Which hardware version (if applicable)
---

# Page Title

**Applies to:** Dragon · Spidar  ← or "All platforms"
**Hardware version:** Spinal H7 v2+  ← omit if not hardware-specific
**Prerequisites:** [Link to prerequisite page]

## Overview

Brief description of what this page covers.

## Steps / Content

...

## Verification

How to confirm the procedure succeeded.

## Common Issues

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|

## Related

- [Related page 1](../path/to/page.md)

docs.title: Version Tracking
docs.text:
When a procedure changes because of a hardware revision or firmware update:

  1. Update the relevant page with the new information
  2. Use admonitions to clearly flag version-specific differences:
!!! note "Spinal H7 v1 only"
    On v1 boards, use `openocd_h743_stlink_v2.cfg`.
    On v2 boards, see [firmware_flash.md](firmware_flash.md).
  1. Update the Hardware Versions table
  2. Update the Compatibility Matrix if needed
  3. Add an entry to Firmware Changelog

docs.title: Version Admonition Conventions
docs.text: Type Use case MkDocs admonition type Hardware version note PCB rev-specific step note "Spinal H7 v2 only" Deprecated procedure Old method still works but not recommended warning "Deprecated" Breaking change Old method no longer works danger "Breaking change in v2.x" Future / ROS2 Placeholder for upcoming content info "ROS 2 support coming"

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
docs/assets/images/
├── hardware/
│   ├── spinal/          ← Spinal PCB photos, wiring diagrams
│   ├── neuron/
│   ├── esc/
│   ├── servo/
│   └── sensors/
├── robots/
│   ├── dragon/          ← Dragon photos, transformation poses
│   ├── spidar/
│   └── beetle/
├── calibration/         ← Screenshots of rqt, calibration setups
├── simulation/          ← Gazebo/MuJoCo screenshots
└── software/            ← RViz configs, rqt screenshots

docs.title: Image Format Rules
docs.text:
Use case Format Max size Photo (robot, hardware) .webp (convert from JPG/PNG) 800 KB Diagram / schematic .png with transparent bg 500 KB Screenshot .webp 400 KB Vector diagram .svg —

Compress before committing: 

# Convert and compress to webp
cwebp -q 80 input.jpg -o docs/assets/images/hardware/spinal/pcb_v2.webp

# Or use ImageMagick
convert input.png -quality 85 docs/assets/images/robots/dragon/overview.webp

docs.title: Referencing Images
docs.text:
![Spinal H7 v2 PCB](../../assets/images/hardware/spinal/pcb_v2.webp)

Images automatically get the lightbox (click to zoom) from the glightbox plugin.

docs.title: Large Files (3D Models, PDFs)
docs.text:
Place in docs/assets/files/: 

docs/assets/files/
├── datasheets/          ← Component datasheets (PDF)
├── 3d_models/           ← STL / STEP files for 3D printing
└── schematics/          ← PCB schematics (PDF)

docs.title: Adding a New Robot
docs.text:
1. Copy the template directory: 
    cp -r docs/robots/_template docs/robots/<new_robot_name>
  2. Fill in each page following the template instructions
  3. Add hardware-specific assembly notes under docs/hardware/robot_specific/<new_robot_name>/
  4. Add BOM under docs/hardware/robot_specific/<new_robot_name>/bom.md
  5. Add the robot to docs/overview/supported_robots.md
  6. Add nav entries in mkdocs.yml under the Robots: section

docs.title: Adding a New Controller
docs.text:
1. Create directory: docs/robots/<name>/controllers/<controller_name>/
  2. Create at minimum overview.md with:
  3. What the controller does
  4. Which branch / external repo contains the code
  5. Who maintains it
  6. Current status (experimental / stable / deprecated)
  7. Update docs/robots/<name>/controllers/README.md with a row in the controllers table
  8. Add nav entries in mkdocs.yml

docs.title: Local Preview
docs.text:
# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Start dev server with live reload
mkdocs serve

# Open http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Building & Deploying
docs.text:
# Build static site
mkdocs build

# Deploy to GitHub Pages (one-time)
mkdocs gh-deploy

# Versioned deploy with mike
mike deploy --push --update-aliases 1.0 latest
mike set-default --push latest

docs.title: Firmware Debug Guide — VS Code + OpenOCD
Firmware Debug Guide — VS Code + OpenOCD
docs.text:
This guide describes how to debug Spinal (STM32H743) firmware using VS Code with the Cortex-Debug extension and OpenOCD.

Prerequisites: Dev Environment fully set up.

docs.title: TL;DR Quick Start
docs.text:
# 1. Connect ST-Link
lsusb | grep STMicro   # Should show ST-LINK/V2

# 2. Open project in VS Code
code <aerial_robot_nerve>/spinal/mcu_project/boards/stm32H7/

# 3. Press F5, select "STM32H743 Debug (Launch)"

docs.title: Three Debug Modes
docs.text: Select from the VS Code debug panel (Ctrl+Shift+D):

docs.title: Mode 1: STM32H743 Debug (Launch) ⭐ Recommended
docs.text:
Use case: Firmware is already flashed; you just want to attach a debugger.

Flow: VS Code → auto-starts OpenOCD → ST-Link → software reset → loads symbols → stops at main().

# Just press F5

docs.title: Mode 2: STM32H743 Debug (Flash + Launch)
docs.text:
Use case: After modifying and rebuilding firmware, flash the new binary and debug.

Flow: VS Code → OpenOCD → reset → flash ELF via GDB load command → reset → stop at main().

Build first in CubeIDE

Build the project in CubeIDE (Ctrl+B) before using this mode. VS Code does not build the firmware.

docs.title: Mode 3: STM32H743 Debug (Attach to OpenOCD)
docs.text:
Use case: Manual OpenOCD control, or troubleshooting connection problems.

# Terminal 1: Start OpenOCD manually
openocd -f STM32CubeIDE/openocd_h743_stlink_v2.cfg
# Wait for: "Listening on port 3333 for gdb connections"

# Then press F5 in VS Code with Mode 3 selected

docs.title: VS Code Configuration Files
docs.text:
The .vscode/ directory in the project is pre-configured:

.vscode/
├── arm-gdb.sh              # Sets LD_LIBRARY_PATH for libncurses5, then calls arm-none-eabi-gdb
├── c_cpp_properties.json   # IntelliSense: include paths, macro definitions
└── launch.json             # The three debug configurations above

If your CubeIDE path is different

Edit arm-gdb.sh to match your actual CubeIDE installation path if it differs from the default ~/st/stm32cubeide_1.18.0/.

docs.title: Debug Features
docs.text: Feature Shortcut Description Breakpoints Click line margin Halt execution at a specific line Step Over F10 Execute current line, don't enter functions Step Into F11 Enter the called function Step Out Shift+F11 Run until current function returns Continue F5 Run until next breakpoint Restart — Software reset and restart from main() Variables VARIABLES panel View local and global variable values Call Stack CALL STACK panel See the function call chain Peripheral Registers XPERIPHERALS panel View STM32 peripheral register values (requires SVD file)

docs.title: Useful GDB Console Commands
docs.text:
In the Debug Console (`Ctrl+``):

# View all CPU registers
info registers

# Read memory at address
x/16xw 0x24040000

# Print a variable value
print imu_data_

# List FreeRTOS tasks and their states
info threads

# Manually trigger a software reset
monitor reset init

docs.title: Build in CubeIDE (Reference)
docs.text:
When you need to rebuild firmware:

  1. Open CubeIDE with the project imported
  2. Project → Build Project (or Ctrl+B)
  3. Watch the console for errors
  4. Build output: STM32CubeIDE/Debug/spinal.elf and spinal.bin

To flash directly from CubeIDE (without VS Code):

  1. Run → Debug As → STM32 C/C++ Application
  2. CubeIDE flashes and starts a debug session

docs.title: GDB fails: libncurses.so.5: cannot open shared object file
docs.text:
Cause: libncurses5 not installed or arm-gdb.sh path is wrong.

Fix: Follow Dev Environment Step 3.

docs.title: OpenOCD fails: Error: libusb_open() failed or Error: open failed
docs.text:
Cause: Missing udev rules for ST-Link.

Fix: 

sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/99-stlink.rules << 'EOF'
ATTRS{idVendor}=="0483", ATTRS{idProduct}=="3748", MODE="0666", GROUP="plugdev"
EOF
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger

docs.title: Flash fails: timeout waiting for algorithm
docs.text:
Cause: STM32H7 dual-bank Flash architecture + no NRST → OpenOCD flash write_image times out.

Fix: Use Mode 1 (Launch) for debugging without flashing. For flashing, use Mode 2 (Flash + Launch) which uses GDB load (more reliable), or flash separately with STM32CubeProgrammer:

st-flash write spinal.bin 0x08000000

docs.title: Debugger stops inside FreeRTOS internals instead of main()
docs.text:
Cause: Using Attach mode on a running FreeRTOS system — it's already past main().

Fix: In the Debug Console: 

monitor reset init
continue
Or switch to Mode 1 (Launch) which resets automatically.

docs.title: VS Code shows "main.c" not found in compile_commands.json
docs.text:
Cause: CubeIDE's generated compile_commands.json is incomplete.

Fix: This is harmless. The c_cpp_properties.json already defines all include paths and macros directly. IntelliSense works correctly despite this warning.

docs.title: Firmware Development Environment
docs.text:
Setup guide for building and flashing Spinal and Neuron MCU firmware on Ubuntu.

Applies to: All platforms Hardware: Spinal H7 v1/v2 · Neuron G4/F4/F1 Host OS: Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
Item Description MCU STM32H743VITx (Cortex-M7) for Spinal H7 Debugger ST-Link V2 Wiring SWCLK, SWDIO, GND, 5V (do not connect NRST) RTOS FreeRTOS Communication UART (rosserial) / Ethernet (LwIP) / FDCAN

NRST not connected

The NRST (hardware reset) line is not used in this project. Software reset via the Cortex-M AIRCR register is used instead. Do not connect NRST, and do not configure OpenOCD to use it.

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text: Software Tested Version Purpose Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04 LTS Host OS STM32CubeIDE 1.18.0 Build firmware (includes ARM GCC toolchain) OpenOCD 0.12.0 Debug server, connects to ST-Link VS Code Latest Code editor + debugger frontend Cortex-Debug extension 1.12.1+ ARM Cortex-M debug support in VS Code libncurses5 / libtinfo5 6.1 Runtime dependency for the bundled arm-none-eabi-gdb

docs.title: Step 1 — Install STM32CubeIDE
docs.text:
Download the installer from the ST website.

Recommended versions: 1.6.1 or 1.18.0

# Unzip the installer package
unzip st-stm32cubeide_1.18.0_*.sh.zip

# Run the installer (accept all prompts, use default install path)
sudo sh st-stm32cubeide_1.18.0_*.sh
# When long license text appears, press 'q' to skip to the prompts.

Default installation path: ~/st/stm32cubeide_1.18.0/

The ARM GCC toolchain is bundled at: 

~/st/stm32cubeide_1.18.0/plugins/
  com.st.stm32cube.ide.mcu.externaltools.gnu-tools-for-stm32.13.3.rel1.linux64_1.0.0.*/
  tools/bin/arm-none-eabi-gcc

Create your workspace directory: 

mkdir -p ~/STM32Cube    # Default workspace for CubeIDE

docs.title: Step 2 — Install OpenOCD
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install openocd

# Verify
openocd --version
# Expected: Open On-Chip Debugger 0.12.0

The Spinal project includes a pre-configured OpenOCD config: - boards/stm32H7/STM32CubeIDE/openocd_h743_stlink_v2.cfg

docs.title: Step 3 — Fix GDB Library Dependencies (Ubuntu 22.04+)
docs.text:
The arm-none-eabi-gdb bundled with CubeIDE requires libncurses.so.5 and libtinfo.so.5. Ubuntu 22.04+ ships with v6 only, so these must be installed manually.

mkdir -p ~/drivers/libncurses5
cd ~/drivers/

# Download the Ubuntu 18.04 packages (compatible)
wget http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/universe/n/ncurses/libncurses5_6.1-1ubuntu1.18.04.1_amd64.deb
wget http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/pool/universe/n/ncurses/libtinfo5_6.1-1ubuntu1.18.04.1_amd64.deb

# Extract to a local path (no system pollution)
dpkg-deb -x libncurses5_6.1-1ubuntu1.18.04.1_amd64.deb ~/drivers/libncurses5/
dpkg-deb -x libtinfo5_6.1-1ubuntu1.18.04.1_amd64.deb ~/drivers/libncurses5/

After extraction: 

~/drivers/libncurses5/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/
├── libncurses.so.5 -> libncurses.so.5.9
├── libncurses.so.5.9
├── libtinfo.so.5 -> libtinfo.so.5.9
└── libtinfo.so.5.9

Automatic path handling

The .vscode/arm-gdb.sh wrapper in the Spinal project sets LD_LIBRARY_PATH automatically. No manual configuration is needed once VS Code is set up.

docs.title: Step 4 — Install VS Code Extensions
docs.text:
code --install-extension marus25.cortex-debug    # ARM Cortex-M debug
code --install-extension ms-vscode.cpptools       # C/C++ IntelliSense
Extension ID Purpose Cortex-Debug marus25.cortex-debug ARM debug adapter C/C++ ms-vscode.cpptools IntelliSense, syntax highlighting

docs.title: Step 5 — Fix ST-Link USB Permissions
docs.text:
sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/99-stlink.rules << 'EOF'
ATTRS{idVendor}=="0483", ATTRS{idProduct}=="3748", MODE="0666", GROUP="plugdev"
EOF

sudo udevadm control --reload-rules
sudo udevadm trigger

Verify the ST-Link is detected: 

lsusb | grep STMicro
# Expected: Bus xxx Device xxx: ID 0483:3748 STMicroelectronics ST-LINK/V2

docs.title: Step 6 — Open and Build the Project
Import into CubeIDE
docs.text:
1. Open STM32CubeIDE
  2. File → Open Projects from File System
  3. Select the project directory:
  4. Spinal H7: aerial_robot_nerve/spinal/mcu_project/boards/stm32H7/STM32CubeIDE
  5. Neuron G4: aerial_robot_nerve/neuron/neuron_g4/STM32CubeIDE
  6. Project → Clean Project then Build Project (Ctrl+B)

Build artifacts appear in STM32CubeIDE/Debug/: - spinal.elf — executable with debug symbols - spinal.bin — raw binary for flashing - spinal.map — memory map

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Debug with VS Code + OpenOCD
  • Flash firmware to board
  • Spinal firmware architecture

docs.title: Project Directory Structure (Reference)
docs.text:
boards/stm32H7/
├── .vscode/
│   ├── arm-gdb.sh              # GDB wrapper — sets LD_LIBRARY_PATH for libncurses5
│   ├── c_cpp_properties.json   # IntelliSense include paths
│   └── launch.json             # 3 debug configurations
├── Inc/                        # Header files
├── Src/
│   ├── main.c                  # CubeMX-generated peripheral init
│   └── main.cpp                # FreeRTOS task implementations
├── Drivers/                    # STM32 HAL drivers
├── Middlewares/                 # FreeRTOS + LwIP
├── ros_lib/                    # rosserial library
├── STM32CubeIDE/
│   ├── Debug/
│   │   ├── spinal.elf
│   │   └── spinal.bin
│   └── openocd_h743_stlink_v2.cfg
└── spinal.ioc                  # CubeMX peripheral configuration

docs.title: Spinal IMU Calibration
docs.text:
Calibrate the IMU (gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer) on the Spinal flight controller using the ROS rqt GUI.

Applies to: All platforms Hardware: Spinal H7 v1 / v2 Prerequisites: - Spinal firmware flashed and running → Firmware Flash - ROS workspace sourced - Robot placed on a flat, level surface

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Connect Spinal to the PC via USB and set serial port permissions:

chmod 666 /dev/ttyUSB0

Launch the Spinal bridge:

source ~/ros/jsk_aerial_robot_ws/devel/setup.bash
roslaunch spinal bridge.launch

Open rqt in a separate terminal:

rqt

In rqt: Plugins → Aerial Robot → IMU Calibration

docs.title: 1. Gyroscope Calibration
docs.text:
1. Place Spinal on a flat, stable surface — do not move it during calibration
  2. In rqt IMU Calibration panel, click Start Calib under Gyroscope
  3. Keep the board completely still for 5 seconds
  4. Click Stop Calib

docs.title: 2. Accelerometer Calibration
docs.text:
1. Keep Spinal level on a flat surface
  2. Click Start Calib under Accelerometer
  3. Hold still for 5 seconds
  4. Click Stop Calib

docs.title: 3. Magnetometer Calibration
docs.text:
1. Click Start Calib for the right LSM (magnetometer)
  2. Slowly rotate the Spinal board through all orientations — roll 360°, pitch 360°, yaw 360°
  3. Watch the point cloud on the right side of the panel form a complete sphere
  4. Once the sphere is well-formed, click Stop Calib

docs.title: 4. Magnetic Declination
docs.text:
Set the local magnetic declination value:

Location Declination Tokyo -0.12666 rad [Your city] [Look up at ngdc.noaa.gov]

Enter the value in the Magnetic Declination field in rqt.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Kill all ROS processes, then press the reset button on the Spinal board.

Relaunch bridge and check IMU output:

rostopic echo /imu

Expected values (robot level):

Field Expected acc_data[0] (X) ≈ 0 acc_data[1] (Y) ≈ 0 acc_data[2] (Z) ≈ 9.8 Quaternion → roll ≈ 0° Quaternion → pitch ≈ 0°

Reference

See also: jsk_aerial_robot PR #357

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Cause Fix /dev/ttyUSB0 permission denied USB permissions chmod 666 /dev/ttyUSB0 or add udev rule IMU topic not publishing Bridge not launched Check roslaunch spinal bridge.launch output Accelerometer Z shows 0 instead of 9.8 Calibration not saved Repeat calibration, check rqt for errors Magnetometer sphere incomplete Insufficient rotation coverage Redo calibration with more thorough orientation coverage

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Spinal is the flight controller used in the JSK aerial robot systems. This page covers the initial setup procedures: IMU calibration, udev configuration, and motor operation verification.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Spinal firmware flashed and running → Firmware Flash
  • ROS workspace set up and sourced
  • Drone placed on a flat, level surface during all procedures

When is this needed?

Just for Spinal standalone operation. You can do these configurations with your host pc and spinal.

docs.title: 1. IMU Calibration
docs.text: Perform the following with Spinal connected to the PC via USB cable.

docs.title: Launch
docs.text:
source ~/ros/jsk_aerial_robot_ws/devel/setup.bash
roslaunch spinal bridge.launch

Open rqt in a separate terminal:

rqt

In rqt: Plugins → Aerial Robot → IMU Calibration

docs.title: 1-1. Gyroscope Calibration
docs.text:
1. Place Spinal on a flat, stable surface — do not move it during calibration
  2. Click Start Calib under Gyroscope in rqt
  3. Keep the board completely still for 5 seconds
  4. Click Stop Calib

docs.title: 1-2. Accelerometer Calibration
docs.text:
1. Keep Spinal level on a flat surface
  2. Click Start Calib under Accelerometer in rqt
  3. Hold still for 5 seconds
  4. Click Stop Calib

docs.title: 1-3. Magnetometer Calibration
docs.text:
1. Click Start Calib for the right LSM (magnetometer) in rqt
  2. Slowly rotate the Spinal board through all orientations — roll, pitch, and yaw in every direction
  3. Watch the point cloud on the right side of the panel gradually form a complete sphere
  4. Once the sphere is well-formed, click Stop Calib, then click Save

docs.title: 1-4. Magnetic Declination
docs.text:
Set the local magnetic declination value in the Magnetic Declination field in rqt:

Location Declination Tokyo -0.12666 rad Other cities Look up at ngdc.noaa.gov

docs.title: Post-Calibration Verification
docs.text:
1. Kill all ROS processes
  2. Press the reset button on Spinal
  3. Relaunch the bridge and check IMU output:
rostopic echo /imu

Expected values (drone level):

Field Expected acc_data[0] (X-axis) ≈ 0 acc_data[1] (Y-axis) ≈ 0 Quaternion → roll ≈ 0° Quaternion → pitch ≈ 0°

Reference

See also: jsk_aerial_robot PR #357

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Fix acc_data becomes nan Redo accelerometer calibration → Save → kill all processes → unplug and replug USB → retry

docs.title: 2. udev Setup
docs.text:
Configuring a udev rule serves two purposes:

  1. Stable device name — Spinal always appears as /dev/flight_controller regardless of plug order
  2. Latency configuration — Sets latency_timer=1 on the FTDI chip, which is critical for stable rosserial message delivery from Spinal

Reference: jsk_aerial_robot wiki: udev

docs.title: Step 1 — Find Device Identifiers
docs.text:
With Spinal connected, run:

udevadm info -q property -n /dev/ttyUSB0 | grep -E "ID_SERIAL_SHORT=|ID_VENDOR_ID=|ID_MODEL_ID="

Example output:

ID_MODEL_ID=6001
ID_SERIAL_SHORT=AL053JRP
ID_VENDOR_ID=0403

Or use the verbose form to get all attributes:

sudo udevadm info -a /dev/ttyUSB0

Note down idVendor, idProduct, and serial — you will fill these into the rule file below.

Reference: https://smdn.jp/electronics/udev_create_persistent_usb_device_symlink/

docs.title: Step 2 — Create the Rules File
docs.text:
Create /etc/udev/rules.d/99-aerial-robot-dev.rules (the 99 prefix means highest loading priority):

KERNEL=="ttyUSB*", ATTRS{idVendor}=="0403", ATTRS{idProduct}=="6001", ATTRS{serial}=="A907HR6F", SYMLINK+="flight_controller"
ACTION=="add", SUBSYSTEM=="usb-serial", DRIVER=="ftdi_sio", ATTR{latency_timer}="1"
  • Line 1: replace idVendor, idProduct, and serial with the values from Step 1.
  • Line 2: sets latency_timer to 1 ms. Without this, rosserial messages from Spinal arrive unstably.

docs.title: Step 3 — Reload and Test
docs.text:
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger

Unplug and replug Spinal. Verify:

ls -la /dev/flight_controller          # symlink should exist
cat /sys/bus/usb-serial/devices/ttyUSB0/latency_timer  # should print 1

If you don't have permission to access /dev/ttyUSB*:

sudo adduser $USER dialout
# then log out and back in

docs.title: Troubleshooting: latency_timer Not Taking Effect
docs.text:
Symptom: After applying the rules above, latency_timer still reads a value other than 1, and launching Spinal produces warnings like:

IMU: bad timestamp. curr time stamp: %f, prev time stamp: %f

Cause: The ATTR{latency_timer}="1" syntax writes to sysfs at device match time, but a race condition between udev and the kernel driver initialization can prevent the write from sticking.

Fix: Add an explicit RUN+= action that writes to sysfs after a short delay, in addition to the symlink rule. Replace the contents of 99-aerial-robot-dev.rules with:

# 1) Create symlink for USB device
ACTION=="add", SUBSYSTEM=="tty", KERNEL=="ttyUSB[0-9]*", \
  ATTRS{idVendor}=="0403", ATTRS{idProduct}=="6001", ATTRS{serial}=="AL053JRP", \
  SYMLINK+="flight_controller", GROUP="dialout", MODE="0660"

# 2) Set latency_timer via sysfs (robust)
ACTION=="add", SUBSYSTEM=="tty", KERNEL=="ttyUSB[0-9]*", \
  ATTRS{idVendor}=="0403", ATTRS{idProduct}=="6001", ATTRS{serial}=="AL053JRP", \
  RUN+="/bin/sh -c 'sleep 0.2; echo 1 > /sys/bus/usb-serial/devices/%k/latency_timer'"

Key differences from the basic template:

Basic template Robust version Symlink rule KERNEL=="ttyUSB*" KERNEL=="ttyUSB[0-9]*" with explicit ACTION=="add" Latency write ATTR{latency_timer}="1" (in-place) RUN+= shell command with 0.2 s delay Why it works Writes at match time — may race Deferred write after driver is fully initialized

After editing the file, reload and replug:

sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger
# unplug and replug Spinal
cat /sys/bus/usb-serial/devices/ttyUSB0/latency_timer  # must print 1

When is this needed?

Not required for Spinal standalone operation. Must be done before using bringup.launch with a full robot.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Spinal connected to the PC via Micro-USB cable
  • Dynamixel servo(s) powered (voltage applied)

docs.title: Launch
docs.text:
source ~/ros/jsk_aerial_robot_ws/devel/setup.bash
roslaunch spinal bridge.launch

Open rqt in a separate terminal:

rqt

docs.title: Initial Configuration
docs.text:
In rqt: Plugins → Aerial Robot → Board Configurator

Configure each servo according to your hardware setup. Make sure to set the External Encoder Flag correctly to match the actual hardware configuration.

docs.title: Verify Servo Readout
docs.text:
In rqt: Plugins → Aerial Robot → Servo Monitor

Check that Dynamixel servo values are being read correctly.

Tip

If values are not appearing, press the reset button on Spinal or Neuron once, then relaunch rqt.

docs.title: Joint Angle Offset Calibration
docs.text:
To apply an angle offset to a joint:

  1. Turn the servo Off in the Servo Monitor panel
  2. Enter the desired value in the Homing Offset field
  3. This performs joint calibration and applies the angle offset

docs.title: Sending Rotation Commands from Terminal
docs.text:
To send a rotation command directly via topic:

rostopic pub -1 /servo/target_states spinal/ServoControlCmd "index: [<index>]
angles: [<angle>]"

Note

The value for index corresponds to the number shown in the index column of the Servo Monitor.

docs.title: Wiring
docs.text:
Configuration ESC connects to Spinal standalone Spinal PWM port Spinal + Neuron Neuron PWM port
  1. Power on the ESC
  2. Connect Spinal to the PC via USB cable (Micro-B)
  3. On boot, each board sends PWM 0.5 — a beep from the motors confirms readiness

docs.title: Sending PWM Commands
docs.text:
source ~/ros/jsk_aerial_robot_ws/devel/setup.bash
roslaunch spinal bridge.launch

In a separate terminal, publish a PWM command:

rostopic pub -1 /pwm_test spinal/PwmTest "motor_index: [0]
pwms: [0.65]"
PWM Value Motor Behavior 0.5 Stop Greater than 0.5 (e.g. 0.65) Spin

docs.title: Platform Overview
docs.text:
[Robot Name] is a [describe: e.g., "6-link transformable multilink aerial robot with dual-rotor gimbal units"].

Key features: - [Feature 1] - [Feature 2] - [Feature 3]

docs.title: Hardware Configuration
docs.text: Component Model / Version Notes Flight Controller Spinal H7 v2 Overview Joint Controller Neuron G4 v1 [x] units total ESC Custom v2 + AM32 v2.18 [x] units Servo Dynamixel XM540-W270 [x] units Upper Computer VIM4 Setup Sensors [List sensors]

docs.title: Available Controllers
docs.text: See the Controllers Overview for all available control algorithms for this platform.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
SimulationReal Machine 
# [Fill in the actual launch command]
roslaunch [package] [robot]_bringup.launch simulation:=true
→ Full guide: Simulation 
# [Fill in the actual launch command]
roslaunch [package] [robot]_bringup.launch real_machine:=true
→ Full guide: Real Machine Setup

docs.title: [Robot] — Features & Capabilities
docs.text:
Applies to: Template

Status: Under Construction

This page is a stub. Please refer to the Contributing Guide to help fill it in.

docs.title: [Robot Name] — Real Machine Setup & Launch
docs.text:
Prerequisites:

  • Simulation runs correctly → Simulation Guide
  • All hardware calibrated → Motor Test, Servo Calibration, IMU Calibration
  • Spinal firmware flashed → Firmware Flash
  • VIM4 / Jetson configured → VIM4 Setup
  • Network configured → Network Setup

docs.title: Pre-flight Checklist
docs.text:
Do not skip this checklist before every flight

See also the Universal Pre-flight Checklist.

Mechanical: - [ ] All propellers securely fastened, no cracks - [ ] All servo connections secure, cables routed properly - [ ] Frame screws tightened - [ ] Battery fully charged and secured

Electronics: - [ ] ST-Link disconnected (debug cable should not be connected during flight) - [ ] All motor spin directions correct (verified in Motor Test) - [ ] ESC armed LED pattern normal

Software: - [ ] roscore running on VIM4 - [ ] Spinal connected and /imu topic publishing - [ ] Localization source active (MoCap / GPS RTK)

docs.title: Power-On Sequence
docs.text:
Order matters

Incorrect power-on order can cause ESC desync or communication errors.

  1. Place robot on flat surface
  2. Connect battery while holding robot level
  3. Wait for Spinal IMU initialization (LED pattern: [describe])
  4. SSH into VIM4: ssh robot@<ip>
  5. Launch roscore and bringup (see below)

docs.title: Launch Commands
docs.text:
# On VIM4 (via SSH):
source ~/ros/jsk_aerial_robot_ws/devel/setup.bash

# [Fill in actual launch command]
roslaunch [package] [robot]_bringup.launch real_machine:=true

Key launch parameters:

Parameter Default Description real_machine false Must be true for real hardware [param] [value] [Description]

docs.title: Arming and Flight
docs.text:
# Keyboard control (in separate terminal):
roslaunch [package] teleop_keyboard.launch

# Key bindings:
# [Fill in key bindings]

docs.title: Power-Off Sequence
docs.text:
1. Land the robot (disarm first)
  2. Kill the ROS launch
  3. Disconnect battery immediately after motors stop

docs.title: [Robot Name] — Available Controllers
docs.text: This page lists all control algorithms available for [Robot Name], maintained by different team members.

docs.title: Controller Overview
docs.text:
Controller Type Status Maintainer Branch / Repo LQI (Default) Under-actuated LQI Stable — aerial_robot/deploy Template Controller — Stub — —

Status legend:

  • Stable — tested on real machine, safe to use
  • Experimental — tested in simulation, real-machine untested
  • Broken — known issues, do not use
  • Archived — deprecated, kept for reference

docs.title: How to Add a Controller
docs.text:
1. Create a subdirectory: robots/[name]/controllers/<your_controller>/
  2. At minimum, create overview.md with:
  3. What the controller does (control law, reference)
  4. Which code branch or external repo to use
  5. Simulation launch command
  6. Known limitations
  7. Add a row to the table above
  8. Add nav entries to mkdocs.yml

docs.title: Compatibility Matrix
docs.text:
This matrix shows which hardware and firmware versions have been validated together on each robot platform.

Always verify before deployment

Using untested combinations may cause unexpected behavior. If you validate a new combination, please update this table.

docs.title: Dragon
docs.text: Robot Config Spinal PCB Spinal FW Branch Neuron PCB ESC Servo Upper PC Validated Dragon v3 (current) H7 v2 deploy G4 v1 Custom v2 + AM32 v2.18 XM540-W270 VIM4 Yes Dragon v2 H7 v1 deploy-h7v1 F4 v2 Custom v1 + AM32 v1.x XM540-W270 Jetson Xavier NX Yes

docs.title: Spidar
docs.text: Robot Config Spinal PCB Spinal FW Branch Neuron PCB ESC Servo Upper PC Validated Spidar v1 (current) H7 v2 deploy G4 v1 Custom v2 + AM32 v2.18 XM540-W270 VIM4 Yes

docs.title: Beetle
docs.text: Robot Config Spinal PCB Spinal FW Branch ESC Upper PC Validated Beetle v1 (current) H7 v2 deploy Custom v2 + AM32 v2.18 VIM4 Yes

docs.title: ROS / OS Compatibility
docs.text: ROS Version Ubuntu Python Build Tool Status ROS 2 Jazzy 24.04 3.12 colcon  In development ROS Noetic 20.04 3.8 catkin Stable ROS Melodic 18.04 2.7 catkin  Deprecated

docs.title: Sensor Compatibility
docs.text: Sensor ROS Driver Spinal Integration Notes Intel RealSense D435 realsense2_camera External Depth + RGB Intel RealSense T265 realsense2_camera External Tracking camera Holybro M8N GPS aerial_robot_nerve/spinal Built-in Standard GPS Holybro M8P RTK aerial_robot_nerve/spinal Built-in RTK GPS Vicon vicon_bridge External Motion capture OptiTrack mocap_optitrack External Motion capture

docs.title: Adding a Validated Combination
docs.text:
When you test a new hardware combination on a real robot:

  1. Add a row to the robot's table above
  2. Set "Validated" to :material-check: Yes
  3. If it breaks something, add a row with :material-close: No and a note
  4. Commit with message docs: update compatibility matrix for <robot> <config>

docs.title: Firmware Changelog
docs.text:
Track significant changes to embedded firmware (Spinal, Neuron, ESC). This is distinct from the code repository's git log — it focuses on changes that affect hardware operation, calibration, or wiring.

Format

Add entries in reverse chronological order (newest first). Focus on changes that a deployer needs to act on, not internal refactors.

docs.title: deploy branch (STM32H7 v2)
docs.text:
Date Change Impact Action Required — Initial entry — fill in as changes are made — —

Template entry: 

| 2025-XX-XX | Changed FDCAN bitrate from 1Mbps to 500kbps | Neuron communication affected | Update Neuron firmware to match |

docs.title: G4 v1 board
docs.text: Date Change Impact Action Required — Initial entry — —

docs.title: ESC Firmware (AM32)
docs.text: Date AM32 Version Change Impact Action Required — v2.18 Initial tracked version — —

docs.title: AM32 Configuration Notes
docs.text:
If the AM32 firmware version changes, re-verify:

  • DSHOT protocol version still matches Spinal output
  • Motor direction settings are preserved (or re-set)
  • Telemetry is enabled if used
  • Throttle calibration is still valid

See ESC Calibration and ESC Configuration.

docs.title: How to Log a Change
docs.text:
When you update firmware on a robot and something changes:

| 2025-XX-XX | One-line description of what changed | What it affects | What others need to do |

Keep it brief. Link to the relevant doc page for details.

docs.title: Hardware Versions
docs.text:
This page tracks the version history of all hardware components. When you work on a robot, identify which version of each component is installed so you can follow the correct documentation.

How to identify your version

PCB version is usually silk-screened on the board (e.g., SPINAL_H7_V2). Firmware version can be read from the rosserial connection or from the CubeIDE project.

docs.title: Spinal (Flight Controller)
docs.text: PCB Version MCU Key Changes Status Doc H7 v2 STM32H743 Current production version. Ethernet + FDCAN. Active Firmware Build H7 v1 STM32H743 Initial H7 design. No FDCAN.  Legacy Firmware Build F7 STM32F745 Original Spinal design. Slower CPU.  Deprecated —

docs.title: Spinal Firmware Branches
docs.text: Branch Compatible PCB Notes deploy H7 v2 Main deployment branch deploy-h7v1 H7 v1 Maintenance only

docs.title: Neuron (Joint Controller)
docs.text: PCB Version MCU Key Changes Status Robots G4 v1 STM32G431 Current. FDCAN. Compact form factor. Active Dragon, Spidar F4 v2 STM32F405 Previous generation. CAN 2.0B.  Legacy Older Dragon F1 STM32F103 First generation. Limited CAN.  Deprecated —

docs.title: ESC
docs.text: PCB / Model Firmware Protocol Status Robots Custom ESC v2 AM32 v2.x DSHOT300 Active Dragon, Spidar, Beetle Custom ESC v1 AM32 v1.x PWM / DSHOT150  Legacy Older platforms BLHeli_32 (off-shelf) BLHeli_32 DSHOT300  Compatible Any

docs.title: AM32 Firmware Versions
docs.text: AM32 Version Release Date Key Changes v2.18 — Current stable v2.10 — Added telemetry support v1.x — Legacy, no bidirectional DSHOT

docs.title: Servo (Joint Actuator)
docs.text: Model Protocol ID Range Status Robots Dynamixel XM540-W270 TTL / RS485 1–252  Active Dragon, Spidar Dynamixel MX-64 TTL 1–252  Legacy —

docs.title: Upper Computer (Onboard PC)
docs.text: Model OS ROS Status Robots Khadas VIM4 Ubuntu 22.04 ROS Noetic / ROS 2  Active — Nvidia Jetson Xavier NX Ubuntu 20.04 ROS Noetic  Active — Intel Upboard Ubuntu 18.04 ROS Melodic  Legacy —

docs.title: How to Update This Page
docs.text:
When a new hardware version is introduced:

  1. Add a row to the relevant table above
  2. Update the Compatibility Matrix
  3. Add an entry to Firmware Changelog (for firmware changes)
  4. Update the relevant hardware setup doc to note version-specific differences
